coredata.dc:title: Controllable synthesis of oxygenated carbon supported palladium nanodendrites for highly efficient nitroaromatics reduction
coredata.prism:publicationName: Colloids and Surfaces A: Physicochemical and Engineering Aspects
coredata.dc:creator: Zhao, Wen
Wang, Tianlong
Wang, Baoan
Wang, Ran
Xia, Yu
Liu, Mengping
Tian, Lihong
coredata.dc:description: Palladium nanodendrites supported on oxygenated carbon layer (Pd/ O-carbon) have been prepared herein through modulating the mass ratio of Pd (II) precursor and carbon dots derived from sucrose without additional reducing agent. Characterizations of FT-IR, XPS spectra and TEM graphs reveal that oxygen-bearing groups on carbon dots play important role in reducing Pd(II) into Pd(0), controlling nanoparticle growth and stabilizing Pd nanodendrites. The as-prepared Pd/ O-carbon presents size-dependent activity on catalytic p-nitrophenol (4-NP) reduction using NaBH4 as hydrogen donor. The reaction rate constant of 4-NP on Pd/ O-carbon with Pd particle size of 4–7 nm almost triples that on catalyst with Pd nanoparticles of 6–10 nm. Pd / O-carbon can be reused for 10 times without obvious decreased activity, showing a long-term chemical stability in 4-NP reduction reaction. In addition, Pd / O-carbon achieves ∼100% selectivity on nitrobenzene conversion and > 95% selectivity for the hydrogenation of substituted nitrobenzene compounds with methyl-, chloro- and amino- groups to corresponding aniline through tandem reaction with NH3BH3 as hydrogen donor in methanol/ water solvent (volume ratio of 1:3).
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Palladium nanodendrites
Nitroaromatics
Hydrogenation

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of carbon dots and Pd/O-carbon catalysts
Morphology, crystalline structure and chemical composition
4-NP reduction with NaBH4 as hydrogen source
Investigation of the structure-activity relationship on Pd/O-carbon
Tandem hydrogenation of nitroaromatics with ammonia borane as hydrogen source

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Low magnification TEM graph (A) and HRTEM graph (B) of Pd /O-carbon; XRD patterns (C) and Raman spectra (D) of C-dots and Pd /O-carbon.
FT-IR spectra (A), C 1s (B) and O 1s (C) high-resolution XPS spectra of Pd / O-carbon and C-dots, and Pd 3d XPS spectrum of Pd / O-carbon (D).

The change of UV–Vis absorption spectra of 4-NP solution after adding excess NaBH4 (A), time-dependent UV–vis absorption spectra of 4-NP reduction on Pd0.05 / O-carbon (B), catalytic activities of different catalysts towards 4-NP reduction (C), ln (ct/c0) ∼t plots (D) and lnkapp∼1/T plots (E) for 4-NP reduction on Pdx / O-carbon catalysts, and stability evaluation of Pd0.05 / O-carbon on 4-NP reduction (F).
Time-dependent UV–vis absorption spectra (A), and ct / c0∼t and ln (ct / c0) ∼t curves for 4-NP reduction over Pd / R-carbon (B).
Conversion rate and products selectivity of nitrobenzene reduction on Pd / O-carbon at room temperature in different solvents.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: Reaction conditions: 1 mmol nitrobenzene, 3 mmol NH3BH3, 1.3 mg catalysts, 10 mL solvent.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The reduction of substituted nitrobenzene on Pd / O-carbon through Tandem reactions using NH3BH3 as hydrogen donor.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: Reaction conditions: 1 mmol nitroaromatic, 3 mmol NH3BH3, 1.3 mg Pd / O-Carbon, 10 mL MeOH / H2O (1:3) solvent.

head.ce:title: Controllable synthesis of oxygenated carbon supported palladium nanodendrites for highly efficient nitroaromatics reduction

ce:author.ce:contributor-role: Conceptualization, Methodology, Software, Writing – original draft
Supervision, Resources, Writing – review & editing, Project administration, Funding acquisition

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Hubei Collaborative Innovation Center for Advanced Organochemical Materials, Ministry-of-Education Key Laboratory for the Synthesis and Applications of Organic Functional Molecules, Hubei University, Wuhan 430062, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Hubei Collaborative Innovation Center for Advanced Organochemical Materials, Ministry-of-Education Key Laboratory for the Synthesis and Applications of Organic Functional Molecules, Hubei University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Hubei Collaborative Innovation Center for Advanced Organochemical Materials, Ministry-of-Education Key Laboratory for the Synthesis and Applications of Organic Functional Molecules, Hubei University, Wuhan 430062, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Palladium nanodendrites supported on oxygenated carbon layer (Pd/ O-carbon) have been prepared herein through modulating the mass ratio of Pd (II) precursor and carbon dots derived from sucrose without additional reducing agent. Characterizations of FT-IR, XPS spectra and TEM graphs reveal that oxygen-bearing groups on carbon dots play important role in reducing Pd(II) into Pd(0), controlling nanoparticle growth and stabilizing Pd nanodendrites. The as-prepared Pd/ O-carbon presents size-dependent activity on catalytic p-nitrophenol (4-NP) reduction using NaBH4 as hydrogen donor. The reaction rate constant of 4-NP on Pd/ O-carbon with Pd particle size of 4–7 nm almost triples that on catalyst with Pd nanoparticles of 6–10 nm. Pd / O-carbon can be reused for 10 times without obvious decreased activity, showing a long-term chemical stability in 4-NP reduction reaction. In addition, Pd / O-carbon achieves ∼100% selectivity on nitrobenzene conversion and > 95% selectivity for the hydrogenation of substituted nitrobenzene compounds with methyl-, chloro- and amino- groups to corresponding aniline through tandem reaction with NH3BH3 as hydrogen donor in methanol/ water solvent (volume ratio of 1:3).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: High activity and chemo-selectivity of hydrogenation reaction of nitroaromatics on oxygenated carbon supported Pd nanodendrites catalyst with NaBH4 or NH3BH3 as hydrogen source in water.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Nitroaromatics, a class of important and extensively used industrial chemicals, are extremely harmful to ecological environment due to their high toxicity, stability and recalcitrance to biodegradation [1]. The electron-deficiency nature of nitro groups is essentially resistant to oxidation transformation. Alternatively, selective and rapid hydrogenation of nitroaromatics into high-valued organic chemical intermediates e. g. anilines [2] and aromatic azo compounds [3] has attracted increasing interest in mild reaction condition, especially in water media. Even though homogeneous catalysts generally exhibit high activity on nitroaromatics reduction, they suffer from instability in water or alcoholic reaction media and cannot be reused in chemical reactions [4]. In past decades, massive efforts have been devoted to developing immobilized metal or metal oxide catalysts for heterogeneous reduction of nitroaromatics [5–9], involving in carbon materials [5,6], porous molecular sieves [7], and polymers [8] as supports. Generally, the particle size, distribution, morphology, crystalline facets of active metals and metal-support connection are crucial to catalytic activity and selectivity, moreover, highly dependent on the surface feature of support [10]. For instance, Ji and co-workers reported the nitroaromatics reduction on Ag / C catalyst and found that Ag nanoparticles with size less than 5 nm exhibited 8-fold higher activity than particles larger than 10 nm [11,12]. Corma et al. found it was possible to improve Pt, Ru, and Ni catalysts towards hydrogenation of substituted nitroaromatics from less than 1% to more than 95% by controlling the coordination of metal surface atoms and the cooperative effect between metal and TiO2 support [13]. Due to extremely high catalytic activity, however, Pd metal catalyst usually exhibits unsatisfactory selectivity on hydrogenation of nitroaromatics [14,15]. Therefore, it is highly desirable to regulate nanoparticle size and Pd-support interfacial connection for highly efficient and selective reduction of nitroaromatics.

Carbon supported palladium-based composite materials have attracted extensive interest on catalytic nitroaromatics transformation due to high activity, recyclability and stability [16–24]. Without surface modification, hydrophobic carbon supports e. g. activated carbon, carbon nanotube or graphene is difficult to interact with palladium precursor, routinely resulting in severe aggregation of Pd nanoparticles on carbon support. Of particular concern is that the estrangement between inert carbon surface and metal sites dramatically restricts the adsorption of polar nitro groups and electron transfer. Thereout, there is a growing awareness of modulating textural properties and surface chemistry of carbon support [18–23]. For instance, imported defects [18], oxygen-bearing groups modification [19] and element (N, S) -doping [21,22] are very beneficial to promote uniform dispersion of Pd nanoparticles on carbon supports. However, the majority of reported Pd / carbon catalysts involved in multi-steps synthesis [18,23,25]. Especially, the external reduction agent, such as NaBH4 or H2, is indispensable to the conversion of Pd (II) species to Pd (0) nanoparticles, which inevitably removes some functionalized groups on carbon supports thus decreases the interaction of analyte and catalysts. As a result, the performance of Pd / carbon catalysts is still limited on 4-NP reduction in water media.

Carbon nanodot (C-dot) with abundant surface oxygen-bearing groups is considered as a promising “green” reductant and stabilizer on achieving metal / C-dots nanohybrids [26–28]. Due to the surface properties of C-dots are highly dependent on the chemical structure of carbon source [31,32], however, several reported C-dots derived from histidine [29] and lotted cream [30] exhibited inferior behavior on supporting Pd nanoparticles, where a considerable amount of Pd nanoparticles were encapsulated by -NH2 and carboxylic groups modified C-dots [29] or severely aggregated on carbon layer [30]. Herein, C-dots derived from sucrose exhibit outstanding performance on reducing Pd (II) species. Uniformly dispersed Pd nanodendrites can be achieved on carbon support by simply adjusting the mass ratio of PdCl4 2- / C-dots (Pd / O-carbon). A series of characterizations including XRD, FT-IR, XPS, HRTEM and Raman spectra have been employed to investigate the formation and structure of Pd / O-carbon composite. The as-prepared Pd / O-carbon exhibits unexpected activity on reduction of nitroaromatics in water and/or alcohol solvent using either NaBH4 or NH3BH3 as reducing agent. The role of oxygen-containing groups on carbon layer is also studied on promoting nitroaromatics reduction in details.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of carbon dots and Pd/O-carbon catalysts
ce:section.ce:para: Oxygen-containing groups functionalized C-dots were synthesized by hydrothermal method using sucrose as carbon source [33]. 40 mL sucrose aqueous solution (25 g / L) was transferred into a Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave and heated at 453 K for 6 h. The C-dots stock solution was obtained through solid-liquid separation to remove sediment by passing a syringe membrane filter with pore size of 0.22 µm (PVDF) and centrifugation at 10,000 rpm for 10 min. The concentration of C-dots stock solution is about 5.4 mg / mL.

Through adjusting the volume ratio of the added Na2PdCl4 solution (6 mM) and C-dots solution with the total volume of 60 mL, different Pdx / O-carbon catalysts were prepared by refluxing the mixed solution at 373 K for 2 h, where x was the mass ratio of additive PdCl4 2- to C-dots. Catalyst powder was collected by centrifugation, washing with water and ethanol and dried in an oven (343 K) overnight. In below discussion, unless otherwise mentioned, Pd / O-carbon was acquired with x of 0.05, and the actual surface atomic percentage of Pd species was decided by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) to be 2.5 at%.

The crystalline structure of samples was tested by X-ray powder diffractometer (XRD, Bruker D8 Advance) with Cu-Kα radiation source at a wavelength of 0.15418 nm. The surface composition was determined by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Thermo Scientific Escalab 250Xi) with an Al Kα X-ray source (X-ray energy 1486.6 eV, X-ray tube voltage 15 kV, current 10 mA, background vacuum of the analysis room 2 ×10−9 mbar), and all spectra were calibrated with C1s peak at 284.6 eV. The morphologies were characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM, FEI G②20S-TWIN) and high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM, FEI Tecnai F20). Surface groups were identified using infrared spectrometer (Nicolet Is10) at room temperature. Raman spectrum was recorded on a laser micro-Raman spectrometer (Invia, Renishaw, UK) with excitation wavelength of 633 nm. The content of palladium species in Pdx / O-carbon was determined by inductively coupled plasma-atomic emission spectrometry (ICP-AES PerkinEImer Optimal 8000). N2 adsorption-desorption measurement were performed on an ASAP 2020 surface area and porosity analyzer (Micromeritics, America). Prior to the analysis, the powder was degassed at 120 °C for 5 h to remove the adsorbed H2O on the catalyst surface.

4-NP reduction was performed in a quartz cell at ambient temperature. Typically, 1.5 mg NaBH4 was dissolved in 2.5 mL 4-NP substrate (0.25 mmol·L−1) to obtain solution A, wherein the concentration ratio of NaBH4 to 4-NP was about 70:1. Pdx/ O-carbon (1 mg) was dispersed in 3 mL deionized water with the aid of ultrasonic (power: 300 W) for 10 min to acquire catalyst dispersion B. Then, 50 μL catalyst dispersion B was added into solution A under magnetic stirring, and the absorbance of solution was monitored by UV–Vis absorbance spectra.

The reduction of nitroaromatic derivatives was investigated with NH3BH3 as reducing agent. Typically, 1 mmol nitroaromatic compound was dissolved in 10 mL aqueous solution or mixed solvent of methanol / ethanol and water with volume ratio changing from 1:3–3:1 in a three-neck flask. Under the magnetic stirring, 1.3 mg catalyst and 3 mmol NH3BH3 were added into above solution, and thin layer chromatography (TLC) was used to monitor the reaction process. After the reaction completed, the solution was extracted by ethyl acetate and the residual water was removed by anhydrous magnesium sulfate to obtain the final product which was quantified by GC-MS.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphology, crystalline structure and chemical composition
ce:section.ce:para: Surface oxygen-functionalized C-dots derived from sucrose feature well-dispersed nanospheres with size of about 2 nm (Fig. S1 in supporting information). For Pd / O-carbon composite, TEM graph shows Pd nanoparticles self-assemble into nanodendrites and uniformly decorate on carbon layer with relatively narrow size distribution in range of 4–7 nm ( Fig. 1A). HRTEM graph in Fig. 1B further demonstrates the special structure of Pd nanodendrites with interconnected arms and the clear lattice fringes with the distance of 0.225 nm corresponding to Pd (111) facet [33,34]. Note that the particle size of Pd nanoparticles is dramatically influenced by the mass ratio of PdCl4 2- and C-dots. As shown in Fig. S2A-D (see supporting information), the less C-dots additives, the larger size of Pd nanoparticles. When the mass ratio of the mass ratio of PdCl4 2- to C-dots increases to 0.51, the particle size even increases to 10–18 nm and carbon support is nearly invisible (Fig. S2D).

XRD pattern of Pd / O-carbon presents three strong peaks at 40.2°, 46.5° and 68.2° assigned into (111), (200) and (220) crystalline facets of face-centered cubic palladium (JCPDS no.46–1043), and a weak graphitic (002) diffractive peak at ∼23° [35,36]. The carbon support in Pd / O-carbon is further studied by Raman spectra. No obvious Raman signals are observable in C-dots (Fig. 1D). In contrast, two distinct bands appear at 1540 cm−1 and 1350 cm−1 for Pd / O-carbon, assigned to in-plane stretching vibration of sp2-C in aromatic rings (G-band) [37] and disorder structure or defects (sp3-C) (D-band), respectively [38]. The intensity ratio ID / IG is about 0.899. According to aromatic cluster size La = 44 × (I D / I G) −1 Å [39], the calculated La is about 49 Å, illuminating the presence of small sp2-C conjugated texture with defects (sp3-C) in Pd / O-carbon composite [40].

The chemical interaction and surface groups of Pd / O-carbon are identified by FT-IR and XPS spectra. As shown in Fig. 2A, the FT-IR spectrum of C-dots demonstrates obvious vibrations at 1020 cm−1, 1210 and 1300 cm−1, 1650 cm−1 and a broad band in range of 3000–3600 cm−1, which are probably related to epoxy group, aromatic polyether, carbonyl group and hydroxyl groups with H-bonding interaction, respectively [41]. In comparison, all oxygen-bearing groups significantly decrease on Pd / O-carbon. The additional strong vibrational bands at 1600 cm−1and 3100 cm−1 are ascribed to the vibrations of CC and C-H moieties in aromatic rings [42], also indicating the formation of π- conjugated carbon layer. A new stretching band at 1940 cm−1 is possibly related to π-complexation of allenyl group with palladium species [43].

C1s XPS spectra in Fig. 2B confirm C-O (285.7 eV) and -COOH groups (287.8 eV) dominate on C-dots surface [44], while sp2-C (CC) significantly increases and C-O groups decrease on Pd / O-carbon. O1s spectra in Fig. 2C further reveal C-OH / C-O-C groups (532.7 eV) prevail on C-dots and two relatively weak bands at 531.5 eV and 533.6 eV can be assigned to CO group and adsorbed water (H2Oads.) [45], respectively. A distinct difference is that that CO group (531.5 eV) sharply increases and C-OH / C-O-C groups (532.7 eV) decrease on Pd / O-carbon. In Fig. 2D, a couple of strong peaks appear at 335.5 eV (Pd 3d5/2) and 340.8 eV (Pd 3d3/2) slightly higher than the characteristic binding energy (335.3 eV) of pure Pd (0), confirming the presence of electron-deficient Pd (0) [46,47]. The percentage of Pd(0) is about 65.1% on Pd / O-carbon. Based on above results, we speculate the adsorbed Pd (II) species are reduced by C-OH groups on C-dots as consequence of some Pd (0) / C-dots nanoclusters formation, and the cross-link of these nanoclusters constructs carbon layer supported Pd (0) nanodendrites composite.

ce:section.ce:section-title: 4-NP reduction with NaBH4 as hydrogen source
ce:section.ce:para:
A ubiquitous 4-NP reduction to 4-aminophenol (4-AP) is selected as model reaction using NaBH4 as hydride source [48]. In the presence of excess NaBH4, UV–Vis absorption spectra of 4-NP solution show the maximum absorption peak red-shifts from 310 nm to 400 nm ( Fig. 3A), along with light-yellow solution changing into dark-yellow, which is ascribed to the fact that 4-NP is converted into p-nitrophenolate ion in alkaline solution [49,50]. The hydrogenation of p-nitrophenolate ion cannot be triggered in absence of Pd / O-carbon catalyst, and the absorption peak intensity at 400 nm has no obvious change after 12 h, though the reaction is feasible thermodynamically because of a positive difference of standard electrode potentials (ΔE=0.67) [51]. However, the absorption peak of p-nitrophenolate ion at 400 nm significantly drops and a new peak appears at 298 nm in the presence of catalyst due to 4 -AP formation, as shown in Figs. 3B and S3 (see supporting information). Fig. 3C demonstrates that Pd0.05 / O-carbon exhibits the highest performance on hydrogenation of 4-NP to 4-AP among all Pdx / O-carbon catalysts, and the reaction finishes within 4 min. According to Wunder’s report [52], 4-NP reduction follows the pseudo-first order kinetic law as the concentration of NaBH4 is far higher than 4-NP. Thus, the apparent rate constant (kapp) can be determined by the following Eq. (1) [53]:
(1)
ln
c
t
= ln
c
0
−
k
app
t
where t is reaction time, c0 is the initial concentration of 4-NP, and ct is the concentration of 4-NP in any reaction time, respectively. Based on linear fitting results of ln (ct/c0)-t curves in Fig. 3D, the obtained kapp value of 4-NP reduction to 4-AP on different catalysts follows the order of 0.9501 min−1 (Pd 0.05 / O-carbon) > 0.4667 min−1 (Pd 0.07 / O-carbon) > 0.4382 min−1 (Pd 0.03 / O-carbon). We calculate knor values of 4-NP reduction on as-prepared catalysts by normalizing kapp to catalyst concentration. Apparently, knor value of 4-NP reduction on Pd0.05/O-carbon with excess NaBH4 is higher than that on most reported immobilized Pd catalysts (Table S1, supporting information). All investigated Pdx /O-carbon catalysts show similar surface areas (17–20.5 m 2 / g) with small difference determined by N2 adsorption-desorption isotherm curves (Fig. S4, supporting information), thus, the excellent performance of Pd0.05/O-carbon should be significantly relevant to morphological feature and the intimate connection of Pd nanoparticles and oxygenated carbon support.

Besides, another important parameter, activation energy (Ea), has also been investigated through performing 4-NP reduction on four Pdx / O-carbon catalysts at different reaction temperature from 273 K to 333 K. The pseudo-first-order kinetics fittings and corresponding kapp values of 4-NP reduction on different catalysts are displayed in Fig. S5 (see supporting information). Based on Arrhenius’ Eq. (2).
(2)
ln
k
app
= ln A −
E
a
RT
where A is Arrhenius constant, R is general gas constant (8.314 J·mol−1·K−1), T is reaction temperature on Kelvin, Ea is determined by the plots of ln kapp ∼ 1/T with the order of 32.5 kJ mol−1 (Pd0.10/O-carbon) > 30.5 kJ mol−1 (Pd0.03 / O-carbon) > 19.5 kJ mol−1 (Pd0.07 / O-carbon) > 18.4 kJ mol−1 (Pd0.05 / O-carbon) in Fig. 3E. The reduction of 4-NP on Pd0.05 / O-carbon catalyst needs the lowest activation energy, resulting in the fastest 4-NP reduction among all Pdx / O-carbon catalysts.

The reusability and stability of Pd0.05 / O-carbon catalyst is also investigated on 4-NP reduction. It can be seen from Fig. 3F that the conversion of 4-NP in each recycling run completes in 2 min, and the activity of catalyst has no obvious decrease in ten cycles. The reused catalyst has no apparent morphological change in comparison of the fresh Pd / O-carbon (TEM in Fig. S6, supporting information). Moreover, ICP-AES results manifest that the concentration of leaching palladium species is very low, about 0.066 and 0.075 mg·L−1 after 5 and 10 cycling runs, respectively. These results indicate an excellent chemical stability of Pd / O-carbon on 4-NP reduction using NaBH4 as hydrogen donor.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Investigation of the structure-activity relationship on Pd/O-carbon
ce:section.ce:para: As NaBH4 with strong reduction ability can spontaneously react with surface oxygen-containing moieties bonding on carbon core with π-π conjugated structure, e. g. aldehydes, ketones etc [54]., we made an attempt to break the interaction between Pd species and carbon support on Pd / O-carbon through NaBH4 with high concentration (e. g 0.150 mM) treatment, about 10-folds higher than that for 4-NP reduction. The collected NaBH4-treated catalyst (Pd / R-carbon) was characterized by TEM and XPS spectra. It can be seen from Fig. S7 (see supporting information) that carbon support is extensively destroyed and Pd nanoparticles agglomerate severely on Pd / R-carbon. The normalized O1s and Pd 3d XPS spectra of catalyst before and after treatment by NaBH4 are compared in Fig. S8 (see supporting information). The peak intensity of both CO and Pd (II) significantly decrease. Moreover, the characteristic binding energy of Pd (0) in Pd / R-carbon shifts to 335.3 eV, lower than 335.5 eV on Pd / O-carbon. These results indicate the strong interaction between Pd species and oxygen-containing groups is curial to stabilizing Pd nanodendrites on carbon layer. After the interaction is damaged by NaBH4, carbon support collapses resulting in severe agglomeration of Pd nanoparticles. In comparison of Pd / O-carbon, Pd / R-carbon exhibits significantly decreased catalytic activity on 4-NP reduction and the reaction completes at about 22 min ( Fig. 4A). The kapp of 4-NP reduction is about 0.124 min−1 on Pd / R-carbon, 0.13 times that on Pd / O-carbon (Fig. 4B). The result suggests the synergy of Pd nanodentrites and oxygenated carbon support is indispensable for superior catalytic activity of Pd / O-carbon towards 4-NP reduction.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Tandem hydrogenation of nitroaromatics with ammonia borane as hydrogen source
ce:section.ce:para: Considering a possible long-term instability of carbon layer using NaBH4 as reductant, ammonia borane (AB) with the known highest hydrogen content, is chosen as hydrogen donor for nitrobenzene hydrogenation on Pd / O-carbon catalyst. We investigate the conversion rate and product selectivity of nitrobenzene reduction on Pd / O-carbon using NH3BH3 as hydrogen donor in water, alcohol or alcohol / water mixed solvent. As shown in Table 1, four possible products can be detected, including nitroso benzene (1), anilines (2), aromatic azo compounds (3), and aromatic azo compounds (4). Products 1 and 2 are main products in methanol or ethanol solvent. The addition of water not only shortens the reaction time of nitrobenzene hydrogenation, but also significantly improve the selectivity of product 2 to > 99% in either ethanol /water or methanol / water solvent. In particular, both conversion rate and selectivity of aniline (product 2) reach ∼100% in methanol / water solvent (VM / VW =1:3). This is probably due to the fact that H2O modulating the hydrolysis of ammonia borane to provide abundant active H to react with nitrobenzene [55–57]. The turnover frequency (TOF) of Pd / O-carbon is calculated to be 81.1 min−1, which is higher than most reported Pd catalysts on nitrobenzene reduction, as presented in Table S2.

In addition, Pd / O-carbon also exhibits excellent activity and high chemo-selectivity on conversing substituted nitroaromatics to anilines through tandem reaction. Table 2 demonstrates > 99% conversion rate, more than 95% chemoselectivity and relatively high TOF values on substituted nitroaromatics reduction with H2 derived from NH3BH3 hydrolysis at room temperature. Different from the harsh hydrogenation condition of p-chloronitrobenzene (p-CNB), e. g. high H2 pressure (up to 60 atm) and high reaction temperature, leading to undesired C-Cl scission and low chemoselectivity of p-chloroaniline (p-CAN) [58–60], Pd / O-carbon exhibits high activity on NH3BH3 hydrolysis and selective activation of nitro- group on p-CNB. Consequently, 95% selectivity is achieved for tandem hydrogenation of p-CNB to p-CAN at ambient conditions.

In summary, we report a facile synthesis of oxygenated carbon supported palladium nanodendrites using C-dots derived from sucrose as reducing agent. The oxygen-bearing groups on C-dots importantly affect the particle size and palladium nanodendrites dispersion on carbon layer. Through modulating mass ratio of additive PdCl4 2- to C-dots, uniformly dispersed Pd nanodendrites with particle size of 4–7 nm are obtained on carbon support, which exhibit superior activity on 4-NP reduction to 4-AP using NaBH4 as hydrogen donor in water with active energy of 18.4 kJ mol−1. After removal of oxygen-bearing groups, carbon support collapses and Pd nanoparticles aggregate severely and the catalytic activity also significantly decreases. In addition, Pd / O-carbon also exhibits high chemoselectivity on conversing nitrobenzene (∼100%) and derivatives (> 95%) to target aniline through Tandem hydrogenation reaction using NH3BH3 as hydrogen donor under ambient condition. Thereout, this study provides a “green” route for developing structure-controllable supported metal catalyst with high performance and chemoselectivity on hydrogenation of nitroaromatics.

Wen Zhao: Conceptualization, Investigation, Methodology, Validation, Formal analysis, Writing – review & editing. Tianlong Wang: Conceptualization, Methodology, Software, Writing – original draft. Baoan Wang: Investigation, Formal analysis. Ran Wang: Investigation, Formal analysis. Yu Xia: Investigation, Software. Mengping Liu: Software, Formal analysis. Lihong Tian: Supervision, Resources, Writing – review & editing, Project administration, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare the following financial interests/personal relationships which may be considered as potential competing interests: Lihong Tian reports financial support was provided by National Natural Science Foundation of China. Lihong Tian has patent #201911125911.6 pending to China National Intellectual Property Administration.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was funded by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (no. 51872081).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Nitroaromatic compounds, from synthesis to biodegradation

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: K.S. Ju, R.E. Parales, Nitroaromatic compounds, from synthesis to biodegradation, Microbiol. Mol. Biol. Rev. 74 (2010) 250–272. https://doi.org/10.1128/mmbr.00006–10.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Recent developments in heterogeneous selective hydrogenation of halogenated nitroaromatic compounds to halogenated anilines

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Pietrowski, Recent developments in heterogeneous selective hydrogenation of halogenated nitroaromatic compounds to halogenated anilines, Curr. Org. Synth. 9 (2012) 470–487. https://doi.org/ 10.2174/157017912802651456.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Visible light assisted reduction of 4-nitrophenol to 4-aminophenol on Ag / TiO2 photocatalysts synthesized by hybrid templates

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M.M. Mohamed, M.S. Al-Sharif, Visible light assisted reduction of 4-nitrophenol to 4-aminophenol on Ag / TiO2 photocatalysts synthesized by hybrid templates, Appl. Catal. B Environ. 142 (2013) 432–441. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.apcatb.2013.05.058.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Environmentally benign selective hydrogenation of α, β-unsaturated aldehydes and reduction of aromatic nitro compounds using Cu based bimetallic nanoparticles supported on multiwalled carbon nanotubes and mesoporous carbon

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H.N. Hareesh, K.U. Minchitha, K. Venkatesh, N. Nagaraju, N. A 8 (2020) 12097–12105. https://doi.org/10.1039/D0TA02725A.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Q.F. Wang, J.T. Wang, D.H. Wang, M. Turhong, M.H. Zhang, Recyclable and effective Pd / poly(N-isopropylacrylamide) catalyst for hydrodechlorination of 4-chlorophenol in aqueous solution, Chem. Eng. J. 280 (2015) 158–164. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cej.2015.05.106.
